attributes.stem:
A 50-year-old man presents to his General Practitioner with a three-month history of bilateral calf pain after walking 200 meters. The pain is worse on walking uphill and was relieved by rest; however, more recently, he has noticed this pain in his calves whilst at rest. He has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus and is on hydrochlorothiazide and metformin for his medical conditions. The patient is a current smoker with a 40-pack-year history.

On examination he appears well. His pulse rate is 80/min, blood pressure 142/90mmHg, and temperature 37.2°C. Both femoral pulses are diminished, and no distal pulses can be felt in the right leg. There is a small, dry, and non-infected black spot on the tip of his right second toe.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has peripheral vascular disease with an acute-on-chronic presentation progressing from stable vascular claudication to critical limb ischaemia. The patient’s three-month history of bilateral calf pain is suggestive of intermittent claudication; however, the recent episodes of pain at rest with gangrene indicate critical limb ischaemia.

Diabetic amyotrophy usually presents with asymmetric unilateral leg pain followed by progressive weakness, alongside autonomic dysfunction (e.g. orthostatic hypotension). The patient’s bilateral presentation makes this diagnosis unlikely. Whilst neurogenic claudication (lumbar spinal stenosis) does present with pain exacerbated by movement and alleviated by sitting, it usually presents asymmetrically. Peripheral nerve compression is also unlikely, as one would expect numbness or changed sensation in a single nerve territory.

This patient has a threatened limb and will likely require revascularisation to prevent limb loss. He has multiple important significant contributing factors including diabetes, hypertension and smoking. Left untreated, this patient has a significant chance of limb amputation within 12 months, while patients with critical limb ischaemia are at a 30-50% risk of cardiovascular events such as myocardial infarction or stroke over a one-year period.

Guidelines recommend measuring ankle or toe pressures; or ankle index to assess severity. Invasive angiography is most commonly used to clarify the potential for revascularisation, indicated if it will prevent limb loss while preserving mobility and function. Options for revascularisation include open and endovascular. Open offers a more definitive treatment, but endovascular offers a lower morbidity and mortality. Ultimately the choice is dependent on surgeon and patient factors.
learningPoints: Critical limb ischaemia requires immediate action to define and treat the underlying cause.
references: Kinlay S. Management of Critical Limb Ischemia. Circ Cardiovasc Interv. 2016;9(2):e001946. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26858079/Kinlay S. Management of Critical Limb Ischemia. Circ Cardiovasc Interv. 2016;9(2):e001946. doi:10.1161/CIRCINTERVENTIONS.115.001946

Twydell PT. Diabetic amyotrophy and idiopathic lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy. In: UpToDate, Shefner JM (Ed), Wolters Kluwer. Updated February 7, 2023. Accessed March 7, 2024. https://www.uptodate.com/contents/diabetic-amyotrophy-and-idiopathic-lumbosacral-radiculoplexus-neuropathy

Rutkove SB, Bradshaw DY. Evaluation of peripheral nerve and muscle disease In: UpToDate, Shefner JM (Ed), Wolters Kluwer. Updated October 11, 2023. Accessed March 7, 2024.
https://www.uptodate.com/contents/evaluation-of-peripheral-nerve-and-muscle-disease
